mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows users to manage security and compliance through policy-as-code. It is particularly useful for teams looking to enforce policies consistently across their Kubernetes clusters. Key trade-offs include the need for ongoing policy management and potential performance impacts depending on the complexity of the policies defined.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Kyverno repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Kyverno with Helm
steps.description:
This step installs Kyverno in the 'kyverno' namespace using the specified version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kyverno

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

```

> Creates a namespace for Kyverno.
> Installs Kyverno using the specified version.

steps.title: Verify Kyverno installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Kyverno pods are running successfully.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Lists all pods in the kyverno namespace to verify the installation.

steps.title: Create an example policy
steps.description:
This step creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's functionality.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

```

> This command creates a policy that requires the 'app' label on Pods.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes Kyverno from the cluster. Be aware that any policies created will also be deleted, which may lead to data loss in terms of policy enforcement.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete namespace kyverno

```

> Uninstalls the Kyverno release.
> Deletes the Kyverno namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your policies. This step will upgrade Kyverno to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get clusterpolicies -o yaml > clusterpolicies-backup.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backs up existing cluster policies.
> Upgrades Kyverno to the specified version.
> Verifies that the Kyverno pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to create policy'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the policy YAML is incorrectly formatted.

**Fix:**
Check the policy YAML for syntax errors and ensure it adheres to the required schema.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if there is an issue with the configuration or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Kyverno pod using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Policies are not being enforced
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the policies are not correctly applied or if there are issues with the Kyverno controller.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the policies are applied correctly and check the status of the Kyverno pods.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'unable to validate resource'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the resource being validated does not match the policy criteria.

**Fix:**
Review the policy rules and ensure that the resource meets the specified criteria.

versionNotes.changes: Improved policy validation performance and added support for new Kubernetes resource types.
versionNotes.deprecations: No specific deprecations noted in this release.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No special migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running in the 'kyverno' namespace with a status of 'Running'. Use 'kubectl get pods -n kyverno' to verify.

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.25 or higher and have kubectl and Helm installed.
